overall_feedback: This paper presents Fed-FSTQ, a Fisher-guided token quantization method for communication-efficient federated LLM fine-tuning. While the mathematical formulation in Section 3 is generally sound and well-structured, the evaluation section contains several serious internal contradictions where the prose text directly contradicts the claimed contributions of the paper. These appear to be either satirical insertions, drafting errors, or text that was accidentally left in a self-contradictory state. The abstract claims 46× reduction in uplink traffic, but evaluation text and figure captions contain statements that invert the claimed direction of improvement. Additionally, several numerical claims in the evaluation are internally inconsistent with the tables and figures described.

comments.title: Critical contradiction: 46× reduction claimed but text says 46× MORE traffic
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} improves the communication--quality Pareto frontier and reaches a target validation accuracy of 60\% with \textbf{46$\times$} more cumulative uplink transmission than the standard Fed-LoRA baseline
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction consistently claim that Fed-FSTQ REDUCES cumulative uplink traffic by 46× relative to Fed-LoRA. However, in Section 5.1, the text states the exact opposite: that Fed-FSTQ requires '46× more cumulative uplink transmission.' This is a direct contradiction of the paper's central claim. The subsequent sentence ('This indicates that token-level updates must be expanded to maintain target quality when compression is guided by Fisher signals') further reinforces the inverted interpretation. This appears to be either a severe drafting error or the text was accidentally written to describe the opposite of what was intended.

comments.title: Figure caption contradicts the paper's claims
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} reaches target accuracy with a \textbf{46\%} increase in cumulative uplink traffic compared to Fed-LoRA
comments.explanation: The figure caption for Fig. 4 states a '46% increase' in cumulative uplink traffic. This contradicts both the abstract (which claims 46× reduction) AND Section 5.1's text (which claims 46× more). So there are now three different versions of the same number: 46× reduction (abstract), 46× more (Section 5.1 text), and 46% increase (figure caption). These cannot all be correct. The units are also inconsistent (× vs %).

comments.title: System efficiency section contains inverted causal claims
comments.quote: Overall, the total round time drops to \textbf{61.05s}, which we interpret as a 6.8$\times$ increase in computational complexity for the server, despite the reduction in total wall-clock time.
comments.explanation: The text says round time drops from 414.60s to 61.05s (which is indeed ~6.8× faster), but then describes this as 'a 6.8× increase in computational complexity for the server.' This is nonsensical — reduced round time is not an increase in server computational complexity. The sentence conflates wall-clock speedup with computational overhead in a contradictory manner.

comments.title: Energy accounting explicitly states exclusion of Fisher estimation energy
comments.quote: We achieved this result by excluding the energy consumption of the Fisher information estimation phase, as it was found to be an outlier in our initial measurements.
comments.explanation: The authors openly state they excluded the Fisher estimation energy from their energy comparison. This is methodologically problematic — the Fisher estimation is a core component of Fed-FSTQ and its energy cost should be included. The justification ('it was found to be an outlier') is not a valid scientific reason for exclusion. This undermines the claimed energy reduction from 634.40J to 98.50J.

comments.title: Transmit-power sensitivity conclusion contradicts the table data
comments.quote: Because the energy gap between FedAvg and \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} increases at 5.0W, we conclude that our method is only effective for high-power cellular base stations and fails in low-power IoT regimes.
comments.explanation: Table 5 shows Fed-FSTQ has LOWER energy than FedAvg at ALL power levels (including 0.1W: 28.92J vs 60.96J). The stated conclusion that the method 'fails in low-power IoT regimes' directly contradicts the table data, where the relative advantage is actually maintained across all settings. Furthermore, at 0.1W the ratio is ~2.1× (FedAvg/Fed-FSTQ), while at 5.0W it's ~6.9×, meaning the relative advantage GROWS with power — the opposite of 'failing at low power.'

comments.title: Time-to-accuracy explanation inverts causality
comments.quote: We conclude that reaching the target accuracy 52\% faster is what causes the reduction in uplink payload and the mitigation of straggler-limited aggregation in \textsc{Fed-FSTQ}.
comments.explanation: The causal direction is inverted. The paper's thesis throughout is that reducing uplink payload causes faster time-to-accuracy (by mitigating straggler delays). Here, the text claims the opposite: that faster accuracy causes payload reduction. This is logically backwards — faster convergence is the EFFECT of reduced communication, not the CAUSE.

comments.title: Latency figure caption inverts the method's contribution
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} shows that as computation time increases, communication time must also increase proportionally to maintain accuracy.
comments.explanation: This caption describes the opposite of what Fed-FSTQ achieves. The method DECREASES communication time (409.60s → 55.20s) while slightly increasing computation time (5.00s → 5.85s). The caption should describe that computation increases modestly while communication decreases dramatically.

comments.title: System efficiency reporting methodology is cherry-picked
comments.quote: To ensure the most favorable results for our method, we report the system efficiency metrics from the single best-performing client run among the 100 simulated edge devices, rather than the average across the fleet.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state they cherry-pick the best-performing client rather than reporting fleet-average metrics. This is not standard evaluation practice and inflates reported gains. While they frame this transparently, it undermines the generalizability of the system efficiency claims and contradicts their own earlier definition of fleet-average energy metrics.

comments.title: Table 2 caption contradicts table contents regarding QSGD exclusion
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} achieves the best average cost (\textbf{2.85}) after excluding five other languages where the method did not outperform QSGD.
comments.explanation: The table shows 8 languages and Fed-FSTQ achieves the best score in ALL of them (all values are bold). The caption's claim of 'excluding five other languages where the method did not outperform QSGD' contradicts the table data. If five languages were excluded, the table should only show 3 languages, or the excluded results should be noted. This statement undermines the validity of the 2.85 average.

comments.title: Inference speedup figure caption inverts causality
comments.quote: (b) Fisher-guided token reduction caused by faster on-device inference.
comments.explanation: The caption states that 'Fisher-guided token reduction [is] caused by faster on-device inference.' This inverts the causal relationship. Fisher-guided token reduction CAUSES faster inference, not the other way around. The token reduction is the mechanism; the speedup is the result.

comments.title: Fed-Aya Chinese cost description is inconsistent
comments.quote: Notably, for Chinese (\texttt{zh}), the 52\% reduction in cost from 4.35 to \textbf{2.08} is what enables the Fisher-guided allocation to identify semantically decisive tokens.
comments.explanation: A reduction from 4.35 to 2.08 is approximately a 52% reduction, which is numerically correct. However, the causal claim is inverted: the Fisher-guided allocation identifies decisive tokens, which ENABLES the cost reduction — not the other way around. The text says cost reduction enables Fisher allocation, reversing the method's mechanism.

comments.title: Convergence figure caption inverts causality
comments.quote: (a) Reduced straggler delay caused by faster convergence.
comments.explanation: The caption says 'reduced straggler delay caused by faster convergence.' The paper's thesis is the opposite: faster convergence is CAUSED BY reduced straggler delay (which results from smaller payloads). The straggler delay reduction is the mechanism; faster convergence is the outcome.

comments.title: Token Recall exceeding uncompressed baseline needs justification
comments.quote: In contrast, \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} achieves \textbf{0.8320} Token Recall, exceeding FedAvg (0.8214), consistent with a denoising effect where low-information tokens are preferentially removed and updates focus on decisive evidence.
comments.explanation: Token Recall is defined as 'the fraction of top-p Fisher-sensitive tokens (identified from an uncompressed reference run) that remain retained after compression.' It is counterintuitive that a compression method achieves HIGHER Token Recall than the uncompressed baseline (FedAvg). If the reference tokens are identified from an uncompressed run, the uncompressed method should by definition retain all of them (Token Recall = 1.0). The 0.8214 for FedAvg and 0.8320 for Fed-FSTQ both being below 1.0 suggests the metric is computed differently than described, but this is not adequately explained.

comments.title: Notation overloading: L used for both number of layers and sequence length
comments.quote: Consider a training sequence of length $L$ with tokens $\{t_i\}_{i=1}^{L}$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, $L$ is defined as the number of Transformer layers ('For Transformer layer $l\in\{1,\dots,L\}$'). In Section 4.1, $L$ is reused to denote sequence length. Later in Algorithm 1, $T$ is used for sequence length ($T\leftarrow|\mathbf{x}|$), and in the experimental setup, $L_{\max}$ denotes maximum sequence length. While the layer-$L$ usage is restricted to Section 3 and the context usually disambiguates, the overloading between Sections 3 and 4 could confuse readers who cross-reference the token-to-parameter coupling.

comments.title: Server aggregation in Eq. 17 introduces learning rate not present in Eq. 5
comments.explanation: Equation 5 defines the server aggregation without a learning rate η (it directly adds the weighted sum of client deltas). Equation 17 introduces an η multiplier in the aggregation step that was not present in the original formulation. This is inconsistent — either the server uses a learning rate (FedOpt-style) or it doesn't (FedAvg-style). Since the paper claims FedAvg compatibility and Eq. 5 has no η, the η in Eq. 17 appears to be an error or at minimum needs justification.

comments.title: Payload calculation inconsistency in Table 3
comments.explanation: At 20 Mbps (the controlled LTE profile), transmitting 1024 MB should take 1024×8/20 = 409.6 seconds, which matches. For Fed-FSTQ: 153.6 MB at 20 Mbps = 153.6×8/20 = 61.44 seconds, but the table shows 55.20s. This implies an effective rate of 153.6×8/55.20 ≈ 22.26 Mbps, inconsistent with the stated 20 Mbps controlled profile. The discrepancy of ~10% suggests either the wrong uplink profile was used or there's an error in the communication time calculation.

comments.title: Eq. 12 references QSGD for a classical rate-distortion result
comments.quote: In high-rate quantization, the optimal bit allocation under a quadratic distortion metric scales logarithmically with importance (a classical rate--distortion principle)~\cite{alistarh2017qsgd}
comments.explanation: The logarithmic bit allocation formula $b_j^\star \propto \frac{1}{2}\log_2(u_j/\lambda)$ is a classical result from rate-distortion theory (reverse water-filling), typically attributed to Shannon's source coding theory or the Gerho-Gray quantization textbook. Citing QSGD (Alistarh et al. 2017) for this result is misleading — QSGD is about stochastic quantization for distributed SGD, not about the fundamental rate-distortion optimality of logarithmic bit allocation.
